title: The Primary and Secondary Corporate Credit Facilities

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This Federal Reserve Bank of New York Economic Policy Review paper (June 2022) describes the design, operations, and impact of the Primary Market Corporate Credit Facility (PMCCF) and Secondary Market Corporate Credit Facility (SMCCF)—the Federal Reserve's first-ever intervention in corporate bond markets, launched in response to severe market dislocations during the COVID-19 pandemic in March 2020. The authors show that announcement of the program dramatically improved liquidity and pricing in secondary markets while reigniting primary market issuance.
key_points: The PMCCF and SMCCF were authorized under Section 13(3) of the Federal Reserve Act to support market functioning for corporate bonds and syndicated loans
In March 2020, yield spreads increased approximately 250 bps for investment-grade bonds and 550 bps for high-yield bonds
Corporate bond mutual funds and ETFs experienced nearly $200 billion in outflows during the last two weeks of March 2020
SMCCF commenced ETF purchases on May 12, 2020, and bond purchases on June 16, 2020, making total purchases of $14 billion
PMCCF became operational on June 29, 2020, but no qualified borrower ever expressed the need to use the facility
SMCCF ceased purchases as of December 31, 2020, and portfolio wind-down was completed by August 31, 2021
why_it_matters: This paper documents a historic Federal Reserve intervention that fundamentally altered perceptions of central bank boundaries. Understanding how the CCFs restored market functioning is essential for assessing future Fed responses to market dislocations. The research provides empirical evidence that credible backstop announcements can stabilize markets even without actual facility usage, informing both policymakers and market participants about the power of forward guidance in crisis management.
market_structure_context: The paper details significant market structure issues during COVID-19: corporate bond secondary markets seized up with bid-ask spreads deteriorating to levels not seen since the GFC; high-yield issuance halted almost entirely between March 4 and April 16, 2020; new issue concessions reached approximately 50+ bps above secondary market yields (roughly double the usual spread). The CCFs worked together as a backstop to reassure issuers and investors, enabling firms to roll over maturing bonds and issue new debt.
important_numbers: $200 billion (outflows from corporate bond funds in late March 2020)
notes: Historical research paper documenting the Federal Reserve's COVID-19 corporate credit facilities. While the source predates TODAY by nearly 4 years, it provides authoritative documentation of market structure during the March 2020 dislocations and the Fed's response. The research remains valuable for understanding central bank intervention frameworks. Authored by New York Fed staff (Boyarchenko, Cox, Crump, Danzig, Kovner, Shachar, Steiner) with explicit disclaimer that views do not necessarily reflect the position of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York or the Federal Reserve System.
